sentences.text: OK.
Yes.
Yeah.
Yeah.
No.
He was one of the crane operators.
And he was--
And his name was Lambert Grutszh-- L-A-M-B-E-R-T G-R-U-T-S-Z-H.
Very important, yes, till, you know-- I was corresponding with him till--
He died last year.
So I knew that Lambert-- Lambert was coming.
He worked overtime.
Maybe he was frequenting some-- he was a married man.
He had a wife and two children in Tyrol who had-- was home with the farm.
And Tyrol is, is an area of Austria.
Of Austria, the Alps.
Not far away from, from-- they were not far away from the Italian border.
Because this is a Italian-- the north Tyrol, they border with them.
And I also knew that he had some venereal disease that I've heard, whatever.
I don't know.
He was coming late.
And I was designated, since I lived there, I was waiting on him to give him-- to warm up his food.
So I conceived an idea.
I slowly-- I was sitting there and waiting on him.
And I started talking to him.
Lambert, you are from there.
You have a farm?
He said, yes, I have a farm and something.
Do you need any help?
So he said, yes, that, you know, his wife could use some help.
And then I started to weave a, like, a net around him.
Slowly, I started telling him stories which were not true, that the chef, the woman, doesn't like me.
I don't feel comfortable there.
I would like to leave.
I would like to work on the farm.
Could he ask for me to go to the farm?
And somehow, he accepted it.
In order for him to take to get a, a foreign worker, right, you had to go to the labor office, right, and request.
I need the help.
Can I get this and this, right?
And we already conceived a plan.
And he agreed to it.
I was surprised.
He was supposed to go-- he was supposed to go on vacation for two, three weeks.
Prior to this, he was supposed to go to the doctor in Krakow.
And he told me he has some kidney failure.
And I was getting a day off every week to go shopping, to Krakow.
I was sitting and hiding, you know, in some other girl's room.
And so I was supposed to go to Krakow and meet him at this address in Krakow.
And then we were supposed to go to the labor thing with him to request for my something.
In the meantime, I took out my suitcase.
There was another Jewish girl in the neighborhood.
There's a cleaning girl who looked like 100 Ukrainians, you know?
She is-- nobody probably would even think that she was Jewish.
And she-- I brought this suitcase, my clothes, to her.
Somehow, I packed it.
And he was supposed to pick it up, right, to, for me.
But this was all yes or no.
I didn't know what will happen.
I just dared.
And one day, we already had this plan.
And I, I didn't mention that I was working in Krakow without the thing and without something.
Because I was also going to the chief of this something.
His wife was coming to visit.
So I was designated as the, the maid.
And somehow, nobody ever stopped me in the street, maybe of the kerchief.
And I was young and small.
So one day, he came.
And he was a little tipsy, right?
The plan was already [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not conceived.
We already had the plan, how to-- It was formulated.
Formulated, yeah.
That's it-- was already formulated.
And I already knew him.
And then I-- he was eating.
I said, Lambert, Lambert, tell me.
Is there Gestapo in your village?
So he started to laugh, you know?
I thought he was [INAUDIBLE].
I said, why do you laugh?
Because he was not the Nazi.
He was the worker, you know, German something.
He, they-- He had something with his foot.
So they didn't take him to the military.
And I said, why do you, you know-- I was not something.
So he said in German, [GERMAN].
I mean, it's, it's difficult to translate it.
I know why you ask about the Gestapo.
I know a long time that you are Jewish.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So my identity-- I broke down.
And I started crying.
So he knew.
And the way he knew, well, maybe I, I have to-- there was another guy, his boss.
He was the chief of the mechanical, whatever, division, from Graz, where this comes-- the guy, the Austrian, the filmmaker.
What's his name-- Schwartzenegger?
Mhm.
From the same town.
He befriended one of my predecessors, the girl that worked, that was the owner of the house, Rose.
And I think he fell in love with her.
Maybe they're lovers or something.
Well, he took her out from Poland, or married her, or whatever.
He knew.
So she, she told him, this Rose told this Otto.
And he was his friend.
He told him.
So my identity was really known to them.
And somehow, I mean, these two-- this Otto and this Lambert-- this Otto, he was such a anti-Nazi.
He was so much against it that when we heard-- you know, I lived through this.
The girls from the village would come.
They didn't-- I lived there in this canteen.
But they were coming from the village to work, from the little town, and always coming and telling their stories.
And they killed.
And there were-- so I, I knew what was going on in this [INAUDIBLE], and this [INAUDIBLE], and things like this.
It was-- of course it was.
That's why I felt that it's time to, to--
No, he took me.
So he knew, and the others, you know?
But somehow nobody-- maybe even the chief engineer, the, the, the, the big shot, maybe he knew it, too.
I don't know.
They liked me.
Yes.
So we-- I left.
I left on a, I think, Wednesday morning.
I went to Krakow on my shopping, right, on my day off.
I met him.
And this was all unknown.
This was uncertain.
Maybe there was 1%-- maybe he changed his mind.
So I met him in his doctor's office at the time.
He went with me to this labor office.
This was in the same place where I came originally through this, where they go through this, help me out, Rita, where they were segregating to send to Germany.
This was this gathering place.
And where they were sending the transports?
The transports, the Polish or Russian.
Not deportation-- This was for the deportation to work, to Germany, not to camps.
Yeah, the transit, yes-- this was the transit.
So I already had this ID.
And we went to him.
And he something.
And I had to leave this ID with them.
Now, there was always a question.
If somebody would something, all they have to do is to check, right?
I wasn't-- I had no birth certificate.
I wasn't registered anywhere.
And so he left it there.
And I stayed in two, three days.
And then I didn't know what will happen.
He was supposed to meet me there with my suitcase.
So I got final.
I went through the same things.
But I came with a German, right?
I got my labor card, you know, based on this.
And I forgot to tell you that the way I tried to defend myself, you know, since my ID-- the [INAUDIBLE] card-- that I was always going.
Somehow, they liked me there in this thing.
And I spoke German.
I was getting little things, certificates.
This and this is working for the war important thing.
And please, the authorities, don't make her stay, whatever, passage difficult, things like this.
Yeah.
So he came.
I was there.
I didn't know if he's going to something.
I didn't know what happened in the office when I didn't come back, right, if there will look for me.
All they had to do is to call the Gestapo.
I've heard that this chief of this something was a personal friend of the governor of Vienna, that he heard and he just-- he saw that I didn't come.
He just didn't pursue.
So was a number of people looking the other way--
Yes, yes.
I managed.
To get to the Tyrol.
He appeared.
He came.
He appeared with my suitcase.
And we went to the railroad.
And at the railroad, there were other people, other Germans, going on vacation from the same thing.
And they recognized me.
So this was all something.
And all right, I was with him.
He took care of me like a brother.
The passage, you know, through Czechoslovakia, it took two days.
Because it was the war.
It was dark.
It was something.
I arrived two days later on a Sunday, early morning.
In, in Tyrol.
Yes, yes-- February 6.
Yes.
We arrived.
You know, was tired, somehow completely washed out.
I mean, this goes without saying.
We came into the railroad station in Imst.
We were like 60 kilometers from Innsbruck-- I-M-S-T.
From there, there was a 12 kilometer route-- valley-- that was leading to his, to his home.
Usually, there were buses, again, going from like this, yes.
So I want to say that I came.
And this is the first time that I saw these mountains with the first snow.
It was late snow.
I thought that I am dead and I'm in heaven.
That was this-- no other place in the world will make this impression on me.
So there were no buses.
We walked.
We walked.
We came.
He brought me to his house, to his wife, right?
And she was this German something, prepared a good dinner.
I was supposed to work with her.
But she called me with this-- not you, but the third person, like in German [INAUDIBLE], right?
She didn't look at me.
She didn't something.
And he was there on, on vacation, right, to doing work in home.
Well, she-- I told him, listen, she doesn't give me anything to do.
I tried whatever.
It was winter.
And she probably thought that I was the girlfriend, right?
And so he said, OK.
You will go to work for my parents.
And the-- he was near the church.
His father came.
They liked me.
They took me to, to t other place in the mountains something.
And I worked all this time for his parents.
Yes.
In that part, yes.
Yes.
There was one incident just the fear.
Otherwise, I was not afraid.
It was complete seclusion from the outside world, complete seclusion.
There were no soldiers coming.
There was nothing.
One day, I was with his sister.
We did all the work like the men were doing.
We were gathering strawberries something on the mountain.
I'm come down.
And I see at the foot of the hill, the house is a policeman.
And again, I remember the fear.
My blood probably ran out.
Because she looked at me.
And she said, [INAUDIBLE], what has happened?
You sick.
You look like you're going to faint.
So I said, all right, all right.
So I thought this is it.
They found me out, right?
So we came down.
And was just some, a matter of social security.
So that was it.
And then the-- I was there.
The American came late.
I originally, I thought I would be the first one to be liberated, right?
But I was very safe.
Just, it was hard labor, which was nothing.
I have letters from his brother and him, that [INAUDIBLE] you don't owe me anything.
And-- So he basically saved your life at the end of the war.
He saved my life.
Yes.
I guess it was probably May 8.
That's when the war ended.
Yes.
They were coming.
I was still working.
But when I was liberated, then I saw them.
All this--
No, because I saw the Americans coming.
But I don't know how to explain it, you know, talking psychology-- something, a complete breakdown.
Now or while I was, I was under this identity or looking to survival, it was really such a, such a-- what, how you call it, nirvana, something.
I didn't exist.
I had another identity.
And that was just something.
Nothing would move me, working or some-- all of a sudden, everything collapsed.
And then the first time that I felt the loss was working.
I went to the basement.
They didn't know where I was.
I was in the basement crying and crying and crying, like everything, something.
And then so that was the end of this suspended [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] somehow.
Animation, right.
So then they wanted me to stay.
This-- the youngest son wanted to marry me, right?
And the Americans came.
The Polish Americans were coming to visit.
Then one day came the, the mayor, right?
And they all knew me in the village, right?
The children were running after me, calling me with [INAUDIBLE], you know, whatever.
And he came.
Unfortunately, their American authorities don't allow you to stay anymore.
You have to go to the DP camp.
And now here came another dilemma.
After all this, the, the anxiety-- of course, you mentioned the concentration camps were so terrible.
But this life with the false identity, you know, it did something emotionally.
It was so draining.
It was terrible.
And I don't know how I always knew what to say and whatever.
But the fear was day and night, never left me, all these things, that I decided I'm going to break away.
I felt that provide didn't hear anything that nobody could survive the way it was going on.
I am not going back to my identity.
So I kept the identity.
So when they came, there were some three other Polish workers there.
So I didn't reveal my-- I just couldn't do it.
I was already-- this was my second, became my second nature, this other identity.
I was ashamed of being Jewish.
That's what I didn't mention.
It was such a shame to be Jewish.
That's how I was-- is for the psychologist.
They talk about it, right?
And then--
I was the victim.
All right.
Now I can go on a little faster.
They went to-- they took me to a displaced persons camp.
Landeck.
This was mainly--
In Austria.
This was in the mountains.
This was mainly for Eastern European, Slavic.
You know, there were Yugoslavians, Russians, Hungarian, Polish, a lot of Ukrainians.
In that camp, I was-- you know, one of the, the men that worked in this village was a former teacher from Gdansk or something.
So he knew other people that were in the camp, prince, former.
There were a lot of Polish people that were sent away from Warsaw when they had the, the uprising.
So this intelligentsia, you know?
So I got into this through him.
And I-- what, the priest were came there.
The priest from-- came from the camps.
I worked as a priest's secretary.
I was taking different kinds of-- there were former, you know, officers from camps.
They were giving lessons.
How long were you there?
I was there a few months, probably.
And all through this time, this Lambert or his sister were coming and bringing me, helping bringing me food.
I was just like a sister to them.
No.
Then from there, I went to Haiming.
And there, the food was already, you know-- the [INAUDIBLE] for the [INAUDIBLE] camps-- the food was not sufficient, I mean, for-- because I got the boils on my hands.
They're giving us watered spinach soup, piece of bread, or you know, things.
But I was-- then I was-- I don't know.
Maybe this was already in the third camp, in Kufstein.
Kufstein was the third camp.
Was the third camp.
And here, I lived with this, this identity, with the people that-- but I didn't hear any overt anti-Semites when they were among themselves.
No, I was taking, again, bookkeeping classes, English, drawing.
And I worked in the canteen as a help.
As a help-- I really supervising or whatever.
So then, again, it started.
People were-- they started to repatriate the people to Poland and to-- he's making the noise-- to Poland.
And then I didn't know what to do with myself.
Came a jeep once with the, with the star of David.
Because one barrack was designated to the people from Auschwitz or whatever-- Jewish Hungarian.
And again, I saw this jeep with a rabbi, probably, a chaplain.
I just couldn't make myself reveal my-- I did not you could wish to be Jewish anymore.
You couldn't-- you couldn't be Helena Epstein anymore.
Was enough.
No, no more, no more-- but it was getting very bad.
It was-- I didn't know what to do with myself.
One day, I got a telegram.
And it was a shock.
My uncle found me.
And he was looking.
And the name, you know, my name-- he didn't know what is something.
They found the telegram that he is somehow.
And I sent him back.
So he-- I had an uncle.
Yes.
And I don't know how to describe.
This is probably what they did to me.
It was terrible.
It would have been terrible to reveal myself to this Polish, this, this priest, and think that I was Jewish.
I felt worst about myself than they would think of me.
So the telegram, I sent back the, the telegram.
Now at the time there, I had no ID at all.
I had no-- I was stateless.
I had no right for a passport.
And at the time, they-- so I have to mention about this uncle, that he survived the war.
He was a prisoner of war.
He was a, a soldier in the First World War in Austria.
Then they took him 1939.
And somehow, he was a prisoner of war.
He came back to Poland.
He was not aware of what, what happened.
Because I found a letter addressed to his family.
They want him come now, my dears, and so on.
But he came back.
And he met some of the Polish neighbors.
And they told him that I escaped.
And he started looking for me.
He went to this [INAUDIBLE].
And the people there where it was-- I don't know how he did it-- they gave him.
And he traced Lambert.
Yes, he traced me.
And this Lambert sent him back a telegram.
And at the time-- and so he was-- there were pogroms in Poland.
And I know that he sold the-- his house.
And you know, they were well-off.
He found some cash or dollars in the something.
So he had money.
And he was able to, you know, come with a passport to Paris.
But I could never go-- had no, no passport.
The only way was to repatriate to Poland.
Because they started to repatriate the Polish people to Poland or to Russia.
The Russians didn't want to go home.
So I was supposed to go.
I took my things.
I went to the train with other people.
I took my books, whatever I had, some possessions.
And again, I just-- the fear of being Jewish in Poland.
I am repeating this, because the, the, the emotional scar.
It never left me.
It never left me.
So then I went.
And the thing was going slow, again, through something.
And my uncle, we are corresponding.
He sent me even some German marks.
And he sent me some addresses of people that survived in Munich.
And so we were going with this train to Krakow, probably, back to Krakow.
And I had some names of people, Jewish people, that would help me from Krakow to go to join him.
And the, the train stopped in Munich for the night.
So I picked myself up from Munich.
I said, I'm not going back to Poland.
And I left the train, left all my things.
And I walked at night to this Munich.
I was never there.
I took the tramway.
I had some money.
I still spoke German.
I found these people there.
They're-- they lived in an apartment.
And I have to say it again, because this is part that I was damaged.
I come in.
And they had a, a-- what is it?
There was no light-- a short, right?
Right, and I, I opened the door.
I said, this is [INAUDIBLE].
I come in.
And they survived hidden in a cellar somewhere.
And the mother is lighting candles, the Sabbath candles.
I thought that I'm dreaming.
How, how stupid are they?
How can they go on with this Jewish, with this something?
It was such a shock.
I didn't want any part of this anymore.
So these people, you know, they found out.
They took a little, little-- some little truck or something they're hired.
They came back with me to the train.
I took some of the things.
And they were shocked, too, this friend of mine, this teacher, right?
You just leaving like this?
How can you do this something?
He took some of the things with him to, to Gdansk, you know somewhere.
He was going north.
And they took me in.
I stayed a week with them.
At the time, my husband was probably studying there, unknown to me.
I was told that there was a Jewish group of Jewish students.
I should stay in Munich, right?
But I said, I had no high school.
I cannot somehow.
But these people helped me to--
They helped me.
My uncle paid them later with some black market.
They helped me, yes.
They put me on the train.
There was no correspondence.
My uncle expected to hear from me from Poland.
Yeah, OK.
Now this will be the end.
I don't have too much--
